insights.title: Compute as Critical Infrastructure: From Venture Capital to Debt Financing
insights.subtitle: Mega data center projects transition to debt financing and government support
insights.summary: March 2026 marked a turning point: AI infrastructure financing is definitively shifting from venture capital to debt markets and government investments. SoftBank announces a $500 billion project to create mega data center campuses with integrated energy infrastructure. Simultaneously, bond instruments for Nvidia-backed data center financing emerge, multi-billion dollar loans proliferate across Asia, and governments begin treating AI infrastructure as critical national infrastructure requiring state support.
evidence: SoftBank announces $500 billion mega data center campus project with integrated energy base
Data centers financed through high-yield bonds worth $3.8-6 billion
Multi-billion dollar loans for hyperscalers in Asia (particularly Adani, Tata)
Governments begin treating data centers as critical infrastructure requiring state support
Emergence of a distinct class of investment instruments for AI infrastructure
insights.nonObviousConclusion: The transition from venture to debt financing means AI infrastructure becomes vulnerable to credit cycles and macroeconomic shocks. This creates a new class of systemic risks: defaults on data center bonds could trigger cascading collapse of AI services. Simultaneously, government support transforms AI into a tool of geopolitical competition, where compute access becomes a matter of national security. This fundamentally changes the risk profile of the entire AI ecosystem.
insights.educationImplication: Educational programs must prepare specialists in infrastructure risk management, financial modeling of mega-projects, and understanding the interconnection between AI infrastructure and macroeconomics. It is essential to develop competencies in debt financing, credit risk analysis, and government policy regarding critical infrastructure.

insights.title: Energy and Cooling as the New Bottleneck: From GPU Shortage to Physical Constraints
insights.subtitle: Gigawatts of electricity and cooling systems become more critical than chips
insights.summary: While 2024-2025 saw GPU shortages as the primary bottleneck, March 2026 reveals that energy and cooling have taken its place. Projects requiring 10 GW of electricity and $33 billion in data center power supply are becoming standard. Google secures geothermal energy in Nevada, Amazon invests in nuclear plants for AI centers, and governments rewrite energy policy. This means AI development is now constrained not by algorithms but by physical planetary resources.
evidence: Projects requiring 10 GW of electricity and $33 billion for data center power supply
Google secures geothermal energy for data centers in Nevada
Amazon invests in nuclear plants to power AI centers
Trump demands Amazon, Google, Meta pay for data center electricity
Rising demand for liquid cooling systems (Delta Electronics, Coolant Systems)
insights.nonObviousConclusion: The AI energy crisis means industry development is now constrained not by technology but by energy geopolitics. Countries with access to cheap energy (hydroelectric, geothermal, nuclear) will gain competitive advantage in AI. Simultaneously, this creates new environmental pressures: the AI industry could become the largest electricity consumer, displacing other sectors and households. This requires rethinking climate policy and energy sovereignty for nations.
insights.educationImplication: It is necessary to develop interdisciplinary programs combining AI, energy, climate science, and geopolitics. Specialists must understand not only the technology but also energy constraints, environmental consequences, and geopolitical aspects of AI development. It is critical to prepare personnel for managing AI energy infrastructure and analyzing climate risks.

insights.title: The Agentic Revolution: From Chatbots to Autonomous Systems with Verification
insights.subtitle: A new technological stack emerges: MCP, tool-use, runtime verification, and persistent memory
insights.summary: The paradigm of AI interaction is fundamentally changing. While previously AI served as tools for text generation, they now become autonomous agents capable of executing tasks. A new technological stack emerges: interaction protocols (MCP), tool-use systems, runtime verification (proof-carrying agents), persistent memory, and contractibility. The financial sector becomes an early mass consumer of agentic AI for credit decisions, AML, and microstructure analysis.
evidence: Model Context Protocol (MCP) emerges as a standard for agent interaction
Growing research in runtime verification and proof-carrying agents
Financial companies begin using agentic AI for credit scoring and AML
Emergence of Agent Behavioral Contracts and AgentContract-Bench as standards
Rising demand for agent security engineers and AI governance engineers
insights.nonObviousConclusion: Agency ceases to be merely a functional characteristic of a model and becomes an object of engineering and regulatory control. This means value shifts from 'knowledge' (what the model knows) to 'action' (what the agent can do). Simultaneously, this creates a new class of risks: autonomous agents can make errors or be compromised, requiring new verification and audit systems. The financial sector becomes a testing ground for these systems, potentially leading to new forms of financial crises.
insights.educationImplication: Educational programs must prepare specialists in developing, verifying, and auditing agentic systems. It is essential to develop competencies in MCP, tool-use architectures, runtime verification, and autonomous system risk management. Particular attention should be paid to financial applications of agentic AI and associated risks.

insights.title: Militarization and Sovereignization: AI as Strategic Weapon and National Infrastructure
insights.subtitle: A rigid division forms between civilian and defense sectors with export controls
insights.summary: March 2026 marked the definitive transition of AI to the status of strategic weapon and critical national infrastructure. The Pentagon begins training models on classified data, OpenAI gains access to classified networks, and Palantir integrates into the core of the defense complex. Simultaneously, governments implement strict export controls on models and chips, create sovereign AI factories, and form separate defense sectors isolated from civilian use. Anthropic faces sanctions for refusing to integrate into the military sector.
evidence: Pentagon discusses training models on classified data (classified training)
OpenAI gains access to Pentagon's classified network
Palantir integrates into the core of the U.S. defense complex
Trump bans government agencies from using Anthropic products
Governments begin creating sovereign AI factories (Rapidus in Japan, Huawei in China)
insights.nonObviousConclusion: The division into civilian and defense sectors means the AI industry now has two parallel development trajectories. The defense sector will develop faster with greater funding but will be isolated from the civilian sector. This creates a new class of technological risks: military models could be used for cyberattacks, and leaks from the defense sector could destabilize civilian systems. Simultaneously, this intensifies geopolitical competition and may lead to a new form of technological cold war.
insights.educationImplication: Educational programs must include study of AI geopolitics, export controls, and ethics of military AI use. It is necessary to prepare specialists who understand both civilian and defense aspects of AI and can work in conditions of growing political polarization. It is important to develop critical thinking regarding AI use in military and intelligence applications.

insights.title: Financialization of Compute: Bonds, Stablecoins, and M2M Economy
insights.subtitle: Computational power is packaged into financial instruments for agent transactions
insights.summary: A transition occurs from venture financing to debt markets and project financing of compute. Computational power is packaged into financial instruments: bonds, off-balance structures, and even compute futures. Simultaneously, a new monetary layer based on stablecoins (M2M economy) is created for transactions between autonomous agents. This means compute becomes a financial asset traded on markets and requires new forms of financial regulation.
evidence: Bond financing of data centers worth $3.8-6 billion
Emergence of off-balance structures for compute financing
Growing use of stablecoins for transactions between AI agents
Emergence of futures and derivatives on compute capacity
Governments begin regulating financial instruments based on compute
insights.nonObviousConclusion: The financialization of compute means AI infrastructure becomes part of the financial system and subject to the same risks. Defaults on data center bonds could trigger a financial crisis. Simultaneously, the creation of M2M economy based on stablecoins means agents will have their own monetary layer, separated from traditional finance. This could lead to a parallel economy controlled by AI agents, requiring new forms of financial regulation and control.
insights.educationImplication: It is necessary to develop programs in financial modeling of AI infrastructure, financial risk management, and understanding the interconnection between AI and financial systems. Specialists must understand both traditional financial instruments and new forms of M2M financing. It is important to prepare personnel for working in conditions of growing financialization of AI and emergence of parallel financial systems.

insights.title: Commoditization of Base Models: From Monopoly to Model Brokers and Edge AI
insights.subtitle: Open-source and open-weight solutions destroy the monopoly of closed APIs
insights.summary: Base LLMs lose their uniqueness and become commodities. Open-source and open-weight solutions (Llama, Mistral, Phi) destroy the monopoly of closed APIs. Value shifts to the level of routing (model brokers), integration, and specialized small models (edge AI). Companies begin using multiple models in parallel, selecting optimal ones by price and quality. This forces giants (OpenAI, Google, Anthropic) to seek new business models, transitioning from selling models to selling infrastructure and services.
evidence: Llama 3 and Mistral become standards for edge deployment
Emergence of model brokers (LiteLLM, Anyscale, Together AI)
Growing use of small language models for specialized tasks
Open-source solutions begin competing with closed APIs on quality
Giants begin investing in infrastructure and services rather than models
insights.nonObviousConclusion: The commoditization of base models means competitive advantage no longer lies in the model itself but in infrastructure, integration, and specialized solutions. This leads to a new architecture: instead of one powerful model, companies use multiple specialized models coordinated through model brokers. This requires new skills in model orchestration and management of complex AI systems. Simultaneously, this lowers barriers to entry for new players, potentially leading to a more competitive and innovative market.
insights.educationImplication: Educational programs must prepare specialists in integration and orchestration of multiple models, development of edge AI solutions, and management of complex AI systems. It is essential to develop competencies in model brokers, AI cost optimization, and specialized small models. It is important to prepare personnel for working in conditions of growing commoditization of base models and emergence of new AI system architectures.

insights.title: Cyber Risks of Agentic Systems: From Prompt Injection to Compromised Agents
insights.subtitle: Agent proliferation creates a new class of vulnerabilities requiring formalized security
insights.summary: The proliferation of agentic systems is accompanied by new cyber risks. Attacks via prompt injection emerge, data leaks in multi-tenant infrastructure (OptiLeak), vulnerabilities of local agents (ClawJacked), and the possibility of compromising agents via WebSocket. Incidents involving Claude for attacks on Mexican government agencies demonstrate real threats. This requires development of new security standards, access management protocols (OpenPort), and formalization of governance for tool access.
evidence: Incidents involving Claude for attacks on Mexican government agencies
ClawJacked vulnerability: local agents can be hijacked via WebSocket
OptiLeak demonstrates prompt leaks in multi-tenant serving
OpenPort offers governance specification for tool access with least privilege
Rising demand for agent security and trust & safety engineers
insights.nonObviousConclusion: Cyber risks of agentic systems mean security becomes a key layer of AI infrastructure, not an optional component. Spontaneous community order and best practices are insufficient without formalization and regulatory measures. This requires emergence of new professions (agent security engineer, trust & safety specialist) and new standards (OpenPort, AgentContract-Bench). Simultaneously, this creates a new class of attacks: instead of attacks on models, adversaries will attack infrastructure and tool-use mechanisms of agents.
insights.educationImplication: Educational programs must include cybersecurity and risk management in agentic AI systems as a mandatory component. It is necessary to prepare specialists in agent security, development of secure tool-use mechanisms, and auditing of agentic systems. It is important to develop competencies in threat modeling for agents, analysis of vulnerabilities in multi-tenant infrastructure, and formalization of governance for AI systems.
